Entity_Description: Dominion is a community in the Cape Breton Regional Municipality, Nova Scotia, with a population of 2,142 (2021 census). Located on the south side of Lingan Basin about 6 km northwest of Glace Bay, it was incorporated as a town in 1906 and named for the Dominion Coal Company, which operated the Dominion No. 1 Colliery from 1893 to 1927. Old mine workings still run beneath the town, which amalgamated into the regional municipality in 1995. Dominion Beach Provincial Park offers a 1.5-kilometre sandy beach on Lingan Bay.

Long_Description:
Dominion exists because of coal. When the Dominion Coal Company consolidated the collieries of the Sydney coalfield in 1893, the settlement then known as Bridgeport grew up around the pits, took the company's name, and incorporated as a town in 1906. The Dominion No. 1 Colliery worked until 1927, and the legacy is literal: old mine workings still run beneath the town's streets. Locals remember that MacDonald High School settled into one of those workings and eventually had to be torn down. For anyone who owns property here, that history isn't trivia — it shapes the ground your {PROPERTY_TYPE} sits on.

Then there's the water. Dominion sits on the south side of Lingan Basin, about six kilometres northwest of Glace Bay, and its best-known landmark is Dominion Beach Provincial Park — a 1.5-kilometre sandbar beach with boardwalks protecting the dunes. It's beautiful, and it's a warning: the same waves that draw families all summer are eroding the coast fast enough that the province launched a beach-nourishment pilot project to protect it. Salt spray, onshore wind, and freeze-thaw Maritime winters work on Dominion's buildings the same way they work on that shoreline.

The housing stock tells the rest of the story. Much of Dominion was built during the colliery decades of the early 1900s, which means plenty of century-old homes that have stood through a hundred Cape Breton winters — sturdy, but demanding. Long before the mines, this stretch of coast was part of L'Indienne, the Lingan area settled by Acadian and Basque fishermen and farmers, and those deep roots show in how much pride owners take in keeping older properties standing straight.

{COMPANY_NAME} serves Dominion with {SERVICE} matched to these exact conditions. {SERVICE_DESCRIPTION} Our work covers {SERVICE_LIST}, and every job accounts for what this town actually deals with: salt-laden air off Lingan Bay, ground with a mining past, and buildings older than the regional municipality itself — Dominion held town status from 1906 until it amalgamated into the Cape Breton Regional Municipality in 1995.

With {YEARS_IN_BUSINESS} years of experience and a {RATING} rating, we treat Dominion properties the way this community treats its own history: with respect and no shortcuts. {TAGLINE} Call {PHONE} or email {EMAIL} for your {ESTIMATE_TYPE} estimate — we respond within {RESPONSE_TIME}.

- Population: 2,142 (2021 census)
- Incorporated as a town in 1906 and named for the Dominion Coal Company; amalgamated into the Cape Breton Regional Municipality in 1995
- Located on the south side of Lingan Basin, about 6 km northwest of Glace Bay
- The Dominion No. 1 Colliery operated from 1893 to 1927, and old mine workings still run beneath the town
- Dominion Beach Provincial Park features a 1.5-km sandy beach with boardwalks protecting the dunes on Lingan Bay
- The area was known as L'Indienne (Lingan) in the 18th century, settled by Acadian and Basque fishermen and farmers
- Coastal erosion at Dominion Beach prompted a provincial beach-nourishment pilot project
